coredata.dc:title: Visible light-driven ZnO nanoparticles/carbon nanodots hybrid for broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity

coredata.dc:description: A Hybrid composite of zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO-NPs) and carbon nanodots (CNDs) was synthesized by the sol–gel method. CNDs were obtained from olive solid wastes by pyrolysis and chemical oxidation methods. The combination of CNDs and ZnO materials led to the formation of a hybrid nanocomposite, where CNDs adsorbed on the surface of ZnO particles leading to an increase of its ζ- potential to be −28 mV; this refers to the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups attached on CNDs, which localized on the surface of ZnO after the hybridization, as well their sizes have been enlarged from 23.5 to 105 nm which is an indication of the successful hybridization of those moieties. Additionally, the CNDs enhance the visible-light photocatalytic activity of ZnO, showing a decrease in the energy band gap from 3.1 to 2.8 eV, where CNDs play as an intermediate energy level above the valence band of ZnO. Antimicrobial activity tests were also performed against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial and fungal strains under dark and sunlight modes. The hybrid exhibits excellent antimicrobial activity within minimal inhibitory concentrations (7.8–62.5) µg/L, and the inhibition growth percentage was 99.9%, an inhibition zone ranged from (19 to 38) mm, and the kill time kinetic was one to two hours. The antimicrobial activity mechanism was also studied by the glutathione oxidation test showing that the broad-spectrum antimicrobial is directly related to its ROS-forming ability with a percent glutathione deficiency oxidation percentage of (88.2%) for dark mode and (95%) for sunlight mode. The findings of this study demonstrate the ability of synthesized hybrid nanocomposite as an antimicrobial active material for broad-spectrum microbial strains and other environmental solutions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A Hybrid composite of zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO-NPs) and carbon nanodots (CNDs) was synthesized by the sol–gel method. CNDs were obtained from olive solid wastes by pyrolysis and chemical oxidation methods. The combination of CNDs and ZnO materials led to the formation of a hybrid nanocomposite, where CNDs adsorbed on the surface of ZnO particles leading to an increase of its ζ- potential to be −28 mV; this refers to the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups attached on CNDs, which localized on the surface of ZnO after the hybridization, as well their sizes have been enlarged from 23.5 to 105 nm which is an indication of the successful hybridization of those moieties. Additionally, the CNDs enhance the visible-light photocatalytic activity of ZnO, showing a decrease in the energy band gap from 3.1 to 2.8 eV, where CNDs play as an intermediate energy level above the valence band of ZnO. Antimicrobial activity tests were also performed against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial and fungal strains under dark and sunlight modes. The hybrid exhibits excellent antimicrobial activity within minimal inhibitory concentrations (7.8–62.5) µg/L, and the inhibition growth percentage was 99.9%, an inhibition zone ranged from (19 to 38) mm, and the kill time kinetic was one to two hours. The antimicrobial activity mechanism was also studied by the glutathione oxidation test showing that the broad-spectrum antimicrobial is directly related to its ROS-forming ability with a percent glutathione deficiency oxidation percentage of (88.2%) for dark mode and (95%) for sunlight mode. The findings of this study demonstrate the ability of synthesized hybrid nanocomposite as an antimicrobial active material for broad-spectrum microbial strains and other environmental solutions.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial contaminations become severe threats to different well-being aspects; that demand urgent confrontations through friendly, sustainable approaches [1]. Recently, many approaches have become indispensable in ameliorating several antibacterial materials [2]. Mainly, molecular nanomaterial technologies are one of the prodigious reputation technologies for their uses in this field. In particular, state-of-art coupling variant nanomaterials have demonstrated dynamic and dexterous tactics, also referred to as hybrid material, as it effectuates synergistic effects of advantages for each associated material and accord with new exceptional features in addition to their intrinsic properties [3].

Promising advances of newly developed metal oxides nanoparticles and nano-composites consisting of carbon nanostructures proved to be outstanding hybrid candidates in wide applications of solar cells [4], semi-conductor-based industry, and energy conversion [5,6], chemical sensors [7], water, and different environmental disinfection and remediation [8].

Various unique semiconductors metal oxide nanostructures such as zinc oxide nanoparticles have been an assuring attractive metal oxide in nanomaterial fabrication due to its immense band gap energy (3.37 eV) and excitation binding energy (60 meV), [9] as well as its add-value in ease of preparation with low cost and non-toxicity, which leads to being implemented for a variety of applications. However, ZnO nanostructures are still rarely implemented in antibacterial materials production [10]. ZnO mechanism relied on producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon illumination by visible light according to the excitation of electrons with sufficient energy compared to its band gap energy [11]. Based on this, it has minor affectivity under sunlight due to the limitation of visible light irradiation. Thus, the visible light from sunlight is not enough to excite the electrons within ZnO's large band gap [12].

Even though these drawbacks can be resolved by enhancing the optical properties of ZnO throughout fabrication with organic materials, organic materials include fluorescence effectively substances in the visible region, where these new fluorescent synthetic nanomaterials include carbon nano-dots (CND) or carbon quantum dots (CQD) [13]. Hence, this type of hybrid hetero-nanostructure can make ZnO more active and increases its absorption in the visible light region affecting effecting the energy conduction band and leading to large photocurrents [14–16].

CNDs are new categories of carbon nano-allotropes produced from several raw materials. Its particle sizes are less than 10 nm with attractive characteristics [16,17]. Their surfaces consist of different functional groups such as oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur [18,19]. These groups play a significant role in their optical fluorescent properties, and ease of chemical functionalization with adjustable physical and chemical properties [20–22]. Indeed, their good biocompatibility and broad excitation wavelength make them good candidates for antibacterial activity [23]. Nevertheless, these CNDs suffer from degradation disadvantages in their formulation, thus limiting their usage. For that cause, one possible way to overcome metal oxide and CNDs disadvantages is synthesizing hetero-nanohybrid composites [24–26].

CNDs can be conjugated with the semiconductor metal materials such as ZnO, TiO2, Fe3O4, Au, and Cu NPs to enhance their photocatalytic activity and thus become able to absorb photons in the visible region [27–29]. Several researchers evaluated the influence of CNDs on the semiconductors' metal nanoparticles and proved that the hybridizing process of these semiconductor metal materials with CNDs can improve the photocatalytic activity in the visible region, as well as the antimicrobial activity results show noticeable improvement of the combination compared to both individual materials [30–33].

CNDs have been characterized by their high photo-induced electron transfer activity and up-conversion behaviors [34]. Besides that, ZnO shows a compatible property as a semiconductor metal material compared to others; therefore, most research shifts towards testing various types of ZnO hybrid nanocomposites [35–37]. ZnO/CNDs nanocomposite is one of the developed hybrids; these two materials are easily incorporated by different feasible green chemistry methods along this line, making up perfect unusual photoactive nanocomposites that can be as efficient antibacterial materials with unique light-induced electron transfer properties to be utilized with sunlight irradiations [38]. The advantages of this combination come with a charge transfer mechanism from fluorescent CNDs to ZnO, causing an altering of band gap and increasing charge separation, enabling photocatalytic applications [39,40].

Very recently previous researchers have proved the antibacterial efficiency of modified ZnO with many types of CNDs in forming different nanocomposites, where CNDs can improve their photocatalytic activity when irradiated with light in the visible region. Gao et al. [41] have synthesized ZnO /CNDs from glucose-forming nanocomposites. This strategy was used for improving the photocatalytic activity of ZnO and thus its antibacterial activity, where it showed excellent antibacterial activity against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria with a minimal inhibitory concentration (6–8 mg/ml). Liu et al. [42] have synthesized ZnO/CNDs from graphene materials and evaluated the antibacterial influence and showed there was no obvious antibacterial activity until it was irradiated with UV photo-irradiation with the enhancement of production of reactive oxygen radical species mechanism. Hojaghan et al. [3] showed that the presence of CNDs from soya chunk in the ZnO/CNDs nanocomposites reduces the inhibitory effect of ZnO alone and presents no antibacterial activity. It is noticeable that the antibacterial activity of these nanocomposites depends on the CNDs source where they play a vital role in the enhancement of the ZnO activity and induce its photo activity.

In this work, the green sol–gel method was implemented for synthesizing hybrid materials of CNDs and ZnO. CNDs have been synthesized from olive solid waste (OSW) products through the combination of top-down and bottom-up routes, with carbonization of OSWs, followed by chemical oxidation with hydrogen peroxide to obtain the resulting carbon materials. ZnO nanoparticles were prepared by the precipitation processes. In this stage, the novelty of this work is to utilize CNDs from OSW with ZnO to have a new CNDs-ZnO nanocomposite, which has yet to be reported. The morphological characteristics of the hybrid composite were examined to study its morphological structure and its photo synergist effect. The research results show the successful fabrication of CNDs from solid olive waste with ZnO and its potential industrial and agricultural applications of such functional nanocomposites rather than classifying the solid olive waste as troublesome waste in unfriendly environmental approaches.

The antimicrobial activity of hybrid material has been examined against various gram-positive and gram-negative bacterial and fungal strains. Zone inhibition, Minimum inhibition concentration (MIC), and reduction in bacterial concentration have been monitored under sunlight and dark environments. The photocatalytic antibacterial mechanism was conducted through a glutathione deficiency test for pure and combined materials to examine if reactive oxygen species are responsible for antimicrobial activity. All parameter tests were carried out under sunlight and dark modes to determine which mode could enhance the activity of the material. On the other hand, the killing time was determined to find the optimal time.
All used chemicals and reagents were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and utilized without any further purification. Distilled water was used for synthesis and antimicrobial activity tests.

Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 with 64 scans and 8 cm−1 resolution by Thermo Scientific Nicolet IS5 equipped with an ATR sampling device. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using Beckman Coulter DU 800 Spectrophotometer between 200 and 800 nm. PerkinElmer LS50B fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to measure the steady-state fluorescence in the emission range of 320 to 650 nm at different excitation wavelengths (300 to 440 nm) for CNDs and only at 360 nm for ZnO and ZnOCNDs hybrid. Nano surf core Atomic Force (AFM) Microscope was used to examine particle sizes and morphologies, AFM images were analyzed by Gwyddion software, and AFM samples were prepared by drop-casting of diluted solution on well-cleaned mica substrates. X-ray diffraction spectroscopy was performed by XRD-7000 model diffractometer using CuKα radiation (λ=1.5406 Å) operating at 40 kV/30 mA with the step size of 0.02°. Raman spectra were recorded using a Renishaw inVia Raman microscope with a Laser excitation wavelength of 532 nm. ζ-Potential measurements were performed by NanoBrook Omni instrument using phase analysis light scattering (PALS).

Carbon Nano Dots (CNDs) have been synthesized by implementing a protocol reported by Sawalha et al. [43,44] with some modifications. Briefly, Olive solid waste (OSW) was used to obtain CNDs particles, where OSW product was washed using hot boiled water. OSW product was pyrolyzed in a furnace in absence of air for 1 h at 400 °C to produce carbon-based material, and then it was milled to a fine powder. After that, 100 mg of the sample was dispersed in 10 ml of distilled water and 0.45% H2O2. After that, the mixture was sonicated by using Elmasonic S 100 H Sonicator for 10 min, subsequently followed by refluxing for 90 min, and the resultant solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min by a HERMLE Z 200 A Universal Compact Centrifuge. 0.22 µm micro-filter syringes were used to filter the supernatant that contains CNDs particles. The prepared CNDs solution was dialyzed through a 1 KD membrane against distilled water for 24 h to remove unreacted material or excess reagents. For further applications, CNDs solutions were lyophilized to get a light brown powder.

ZnO nanoparticles were synthesized by a modified precipitation method as reported in the literature [45]. Briefly, 50 ml of 1 M of sodium hydroxide NaOH was slowly added dropwise to a 1.4 g of Zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnOSO4·7H2O) in isopropanol solution under continuous stirring for 12 h resulting in the formation of a white suspension. After that, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min by a HERMLE Z 200 A Universal Compact Centrifuge, washed with distilled deionized water three times by adding 50 ml each time, and centrifuged again at 4000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the product was recovered and dried in an oven for 24 h at 100 °C until dried white-colored ZnO NPs obtained for further use.
The hybridization approach of ZnO–CNDs nanocomposite was based on a procedure previously reported in the literature [8,26] with modifications as follows:

The two prepared materials; CNDs and ZnO were used in the preparation of hybrid material according to a prepared green sol–gel method in one pot reaction. A set of 50 ml round bottom flask containing ZnO (70 mg, 0.885 mmol) dispersed in 10 ml isopropanol and stirred for 15 min till dissolved. Subsequently, 9 ml of CNDs solution (1 mg/ml) was added to the flask and wrapped with aluminum foil to prevent illumination. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min at 80 °C by using Elmasonic S 100 H Sonicator. Then, the mixture was lifted stirring at room temperature overnight. The obtained precipitate was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min by a HERMLE Z 200 A Universal Compact Centrifuge, washed with 30 ml of distilled water to remove unreacted CNDs. The obtained wet pale-yellow colored powder was dried in an oven for 2 h at 60 °C. The obtained product was referred to as Hybrid for increasing the absorption in the visible light region and the influential energy conduction band leading to massive photocurrents was kept for further characterizations and application.

Antimicrobial activity of ZnO–NPs, CNDs, and Hybrid was evaluated against different gram-positive and gram-negative bacterial and fungal strains such as Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922), Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 25923), methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) (DPC 5645), Klebsiella pneumonia (ATCC 13883), Proteus vulgaris (ATCC 13315), Aspergillus fumigatus (ID18), Saccharomyces cerevisiae (PMID 8849441).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of antimicrobial agent stock solution
ce:section.ce:para: An antimicrobial agent stock solution for each ZnO–NPs, CNDs, and Hybrid material was prepared using sterile 13×100-mm test tubes. The concentration of each prepared stock solution was 1000 µg/ml of 10% (v/v) DMSO. The solutions were prepared aseptically because microbial contamination is rare. Then, a dilution series was made through taken 0.5 ml from each tube and subsequently adding to another test tube that already contained 0.5 ml of 10% (v/v) DMSO to have a final volume of 1 ml [46]. Seven dilution sets were made with serially diluted concentrations. A 100 µl from each diluted stock solution was preserved for antibacterial activity examination. The trails were implemented as triplicate for all materials and its stock solution for both dark and sunlight modes.

0.5 McFarland reference solution and microbial suspension were prepared according to the Clinical Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) protocol [47]. A microbial suspension for each bacterial and fungal strain was carried out by taking two counted colonies from a cultured plate and transferring them to a sterile saline solution. 0.5 McFarland reference solution was prepared by mixing 0.5 ml of 1.167% (w/v) BaCl2H2O with 99.5 ml of 1% (v/v) H2SO4 and wrapped with aluminum foil for further usage. The turbidity of bacterial suspension was adjusted according to prepared 0.5 McFarland referred solutions, and absorbance of turbidity was measured at λ= 630 nm using pure water used as a reference blank. The turbidity scale was (0.08–0.1) which is correlated to a bacterial concentration of 1.5 × 108 CFU/ml. After that, aliquots of 100 µl of the adjusted inoculum were added to each well, considering the positive and negative control wells, and incubated overnight at 24 °C [46].

The agar diffusion test was carried out for ZnO, CNDs, and hybrid materials to determine the suitable active antibacterial concentration under dark and sunlight modes. A bacterial suspension with a concentration of 1.5 × 108 CFU/ml was sub-cultured on an agar plate. An aliquot of 100 µl of 1 mg/ml of each material was added separately to an agar plate with certain microbial strains with a distance of 8 mm. Plates were incubated at 24 °C for 24 h, for each mode the sample was tested in duplicate for each, then the inhibition zones were determined for each tested sample [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC)
ce:section.ce:para: The broth microdilution method assessed the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) according to the CLSI guidelines [47]. A 96-well plate was used for stock antibacterial solutions; each well was labeled with certain microbial strains, solution names, and concentrations. Then, aliquots of 100 µl of bacterial suspensions were pipetted, and aliquots of 100 µl of diluted antimicrobial solution were in serial order according to the labeled well, except for negative and positive control wells. For the dark environment, the plates were covered by aluminum foil and incubated at 24 °C for 24 h; whereas, for the sunlight environment plates were incubated under sunlight irradiations at 24 °C for 24 h. The microdilution method was conducted in triplicate for each sample and mode. The (MIC) was determined to as the lowest active antimicrobial concentration for the tested solution that was able to inhibit microbial growth [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of bacterial colonies forming unit reduction (CFU)
ce:section.ce:para: The surface plating method was used to determine the viable colonies number using the traditional method for all tested antimicrobial solutions at a constant concentration. Briefly, the bacterial suspensions were diluted to 106 CFU/ml in a sterile 0.9% saline solution. Aliquots of 100 µl of diluted antimicrobial solution were surface-plated on LB agar plates with an equal amount of 100 µl bacteria suspension. After incubation at 24 °C for 24 h with continual agitation, the numbers of colonies on the plates for all samples and controls were counted, and the viable cell numbers were calculated as log and percentage colony-forming units [46].

A 255 µl of each ZnO, CNDs, and the hybrid solution was dispersed separately in a prepared 50 mM bicarbonate buffer with a certain pH of 8.6. Then, to initiate the oxidation process 225 µl of 0.4 mM of Glutathione (GSH) was added to each solution. Negative control was set using a GSH solution, and positive control was about 0.4 mM of GSH oxidation with 1 mM H2O2. All samples were conducted in triplicate and wrapped with aluminum foil. The samples were agitated in a water bath shaker for 2 h with a speed of 150 rpm. After that, a 15 µl of 100 mM DNTB Ellman's reagent and 780 µl of prepared 0.05 M Tris- HCl were added to each sample and measured its absorbance at λ= 412 nm [46,48].
The GSH oxidation measurement was calculated as follows: loss of GSH % = (abs. of negative control - abs. of the sample)/abs. of negative control × 100

The time-kill of microbial inhibition assay for each sample was conducted according to previously reported methods [49,50]. The MIC for each tested sample was used in this section to determine the time of microbial inhibition. These concentrations were mixed with adjusted microbial inoculums (10 6 CFU/ml) separately to a total final 1 ml volume, then agitated with 200 rpm and incubated at 24 °C. A 100 µl of each sample was transferred onto a plate and the turbidity of each bacterial strain was observed at each time interval for 0, 2, 4, 6, and 8 h. The experiment was conducted in triplicate. Finally, the microbial strains have been plotted against the time of killing for each microbial strain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of ZnO, CNDs, and ZnO–CNDs hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO nanoparticles were synthesized by the direct precipitation method. The simple precipitation method was based on a previously published protocol [45] and obtained a white product with a yield of nearly 68%. At the same time, the CNDs synthesis consists of combined bottom-up and top-down methods depending on pyrolysis and oxidation routes. The olive solid waste product as a source of CNDs was carbonized and oxidized to obtain CNDs with a yield of about 10%. On the other hand, a sol–gel method was used for a synergistic combination of organic CNDs components and inorganic ZnO components at the nanometer molecular level, resulting in a pale-yellowish powder product of 84.8% yield. During the synthesis route, it is thought that a hybrid composite was formed by the adsorption of CNDs particles on the ZnO surface. Along with this, the CNDs create a weak linkage interaction between ZnO particles (might be van der Waals or hydrogen bonds) [51] leading to agglomerated appearance with large particles in the nanoscale. The followed synthesis procedure and supposed formed structure are illustrated in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1 respectively.

The morphology and size of the prepared materials; CNDs, ZnO, and hybrid were studied by AFM. As shown in Fig. 2 , the CNDs nanoparticles were well dispersed having a size range of 0.84–5 nm with an average size of 2.3 ± 0.9 nm.

For ZnO nanoparticles, they show a size range of 10 to 30 nm with an average of 23.5 ± 3.2 nm. Meanwhile, hybrid materials were in the range of 40 to 180 nm, averaging 105 ± 26 nm. It is noteworthy that the average size of the hybrid is larger than ZnO and CNDs, which corresponds to two main reasons: the presence of CNDs adsorbed on the surface of ZnO particles, and an agglomeration phenomenon shows an electrostatic interaction between hybrid particles, as illustrated in AFM images (Fig. 3 ). The results inferred that this method has successfully prepared a nanocomposite contains both CNDs and ZnO. Moreover, the hybrid material shows good dispersion in water, forming a homogenous suspension solution.

To further confirm the combination between CNDs and ZnO nanoparticles, XRD patterns of ZnO, CNDs and hybrid crystal structures are illustrated in Fig. 4 a. For the ZnO case, the XRD pattern confirms the typical hexagonal Wurtzite crystal structure, with characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ° = 32°, 34.68°, and 36.53° corresponded to (100), (002), and (101) crystal planes, respectively. Other peaks appeared at 47.87°, and 57° which are related to (102), and (110) planes respectively [52]. The CND's XRD spectrum shows a diffraction peak at 2θ° = 25.6° which corresponds to (002) graphitic plane [53]. After ZnO have been combined with CNDs, minor changes in the XRD patterns of a hybrid compared to pure ZnO are noticed, with an appearance of a new weak peak at 25.6° that is related to (002) graphitic plane originating from the amorphous CNDs, in addition to that, slight shifts in the main diffraction lines to lower degrees and an increase in full width of half maximum (FWHM) for 2θ° from 0.53° to 0.64° of (101) crystal plane have been observed, these changes would indicate the adsorption of CNDs on the ZnO lattice components [54]. Furthermore, the broadening of the (101) diffraction peak leads to a smaller crystallite size of 13.64 nm compared to 16.48 of ZnO as calculated from the Debye-Scherrer equation (Eq. (1)) for (101) of hexagonal wurtzite ZnO and ZnO–CNDs hybrid.

Where λ is the wavelength of the X-ray (λ = 1.5406 Å), k = 0.94, Ө is the half-diffraction angle of (101) and β is the FWHM in radius[55].

This decrease in crystallite size is due to the combination of ZnO with the amorphous graphitic CNDs. The peaks of hybrid are centered at 2θ°= 31.77°, 34.47°, 36.25° which correspond to (100), (002), (101), respectively, and can be well correlated to those of the hexagonal shape structure. However, the slight shift in the hybrid peaks compared to ZnO can indicate a change in ZnO lattice constants and a good interaction with CNDs [56,57]. The lattice constants a, b, c and the plane d-spacing d can be calculated using Eq. (2) and Bragg's Equation (Eq. (3)) [58]:

The values of a, b, c, and d for ZnO and ZnO–CNDs hybrid are summarized in Table (1) below:

The modification (increase) in lattice constants shown in Table 1 is referred to the presence of CNDs and also the enlarged d-spacing could propose a relatively high density of ZnO stabilized CNDs hybrid [59].

Chemical functional groups were acquired by the FT-IR technique. The FT-IR spectra of CNDs, ZnO, and hybrid are illustrated in Fig. 4b. The CNDs spectrum shows functional groups bands at 3300, 2810, 1600, 1390, and1070 cm−1, which correspond to OH/NH, CH, CO/CC, CH (binding), and COC respectively [44,59]. The existence of oxygenated groups on the surface of synthesized CNDs is highly responsible for their water solubility [44,60].
While the ZnO spectrum has prominent characteristic bands at 550 cm−1, 698 cm−1, 920 cm−1, and 3238 cm−1 correspond to the ZnO bond for the formation of ZnO [3,8,23,48].

The hybrid FT-IR spectrum reflects the combination that occurred between ZnO and CNDs by the existence of bands originating from CNDs with minor shifts such as 2818, 2900, 1630, and 1375 cm−1 which are related to CH (stretching), CO/CC and CH (binding) respectively, in addition to those bands belong to ZnO as 550, 780 and 930 cm−1. The shift occurs for the 698 band to 780 cm−1 and the broadening of the 930 cm−1 are due to the linkage of ZnOC [27].

Moreover, it is worth bearing in mind that the presence of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on CNDs surface plays a vital role in the linkage with ZnO in the nanocomposite, giving excellent dispersion and high stability in water in addition to an increase of its negative charge ζ-potential from −20±1.5 to −28±2.1 mV in phosphate buffer solution (PBS @ pH≈7.4).

Moreover, the RAMAN spectrum as in Fig. S3 proves the success of ZnOCNDs hybridization as indicated by the existence of D and G bands at approximately 1390 and 1600 cm−1 in the hybrid which originated from the graphitic CNDs structure.

The optical properties of prepared CNDs, ZnO, and ZnO–CNDs hybrid were studied by performing UV–vis spectral acquisition. Synthesized CNDs solution is pale yellow and transparent under daylight. The absorption UV–vis spectrum of synthesized CNDs (Fig. 5 a) exhibits strong absorption in the UV region with a shoulder at 225 nm which could be accredited to π-π* of aromatic sp2 hybridized carbon core. [61,62] Besides that, the CNDs spectrum shows a tail extending towards the visible region [63,64].

On other hand, the ZnO UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 5a) shows only an absorption band at around 370 nm with a cut-off edge at 398 nm which would be in accordance with its high energy band gap [65]. However, the ZnO–CNDs indicate an absorption spectrum combining the features of CNDs and ZnO UV–vis absorption behavior (an absorption peak at 370 nm and strong absorption in UV region) in addition to significant absorption intensity in visible and near-infrared regions that could serve as an indication of the contribution of CNDs in enhancing visible light harvesting of the hybrid composite [66].

The direct band gap of ZnO and ZnO–CNDs hybrid can be estimated by Tauc's plot by performing the equation (αhν)2 = B(hν-Eg), where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy (hν = 1240/λ) λ is the wavelength, B is constant and Eg is the band gap energy. Then by plotting (αhν)2 versus hν as in Fig. 5b. (recalculated from UV–vis spectra) with a linear extrapolation to hν at (αhν)2 = 0, the band gap energy could be found [67,68]. ZnO nanoparticles exhibit a large band gap (3.1 eV) due to edge cut-off and the absence of visible light absorption. Furthermore, the combination of ZnO and CNDs forming a hybrid composite would establish an intermediate energy level above the valence band of ZnO and decrease the band gap to 2.8 eV [69]. This decrease in band gap would be an indication of ZnO and CNDs association and could allow the ZnO to increase its visible light photocatalytic activity and consider the hybrid as a more efficient antimicrobial agent.

Furthermore, the fluorescence emission of the synthesized CNDs is excitation dependent as shown in Fig. 6 with a maximum emission at around 450 nm when excited by 360 nm wavelength. After that, the emission exhibited a red shift when the excitation wavelength increased with lower emission values. This behavior is a well-known phenomenon of CNDs and could be related to surface defects, functional groups, and different energy levels of emissive traps [70]. On the other hand, ZnO nanoparticles show only an emission at 570 nm when excited at 360 nm which could be attributed to stoichiometry and structural defects, along with interstitial zinc and zinc vacancies [71]. Meanwhile, the hybridization of ZnO and CNDs resulted in quenching of the CNDs’ fluorescence due to electron transfer from ZnO to CNDs, [72] Moreover, a slight shift of ZnO fluorescence to 545 nm and an increase in FWHM in addition to a slight enhancement in fluorescence emission have been noticed which could be due to interfacial contact between CNDs and ZnO that plays a part of the establishment of more surface defects [33].

The antimicrobial activity of ZnO, CNDs, and Hybrid materials was examined to disclose the effect on microbial strains using different approaches such as minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), agar diffusion disk (inhibition zone), and reduction of colony-forming units. These approaches were carried out under dark and sunlight modes, and materials deviate in their role as antimicrobial activity.

Disk diffusion methods reveal the varied antimicrobial activity of materials qualitatively. Zone inhibition was assessed for synthesized particles under dark and sunlight modes, as shown in Table 2 . ZnO nanoparticles show better antimicrobial activity under sunlight than in dark mode. In the dark condition, its inhibition zone ranged from (14 to 26) mm; a clear inhibitory zone ranging from (21 to 33) mm was observed under photo-irradiation conditions.

On the other hand, CNDs exhibit low antimicrobial activity than ZnO material. For dark mode, its inhibition zone ranged from (13 to 23) mm, and in sunlight mode, its inhibition zones ranged from (19 to 30) mm, which indicates more qualitative results of the discrepancy of inhibitory effect for the tested microbial series compared to CNDs and ZnO.

Undoubtedly, the synergistic effect of hybrid material plays a vital role in the antimicrobial effect, where its efficiency varies between dark and sunlight conditions. In dark mode, zone inhibition ranged from (15 to 31) mm. In sunlight mode, an evident zone inhibition ranged from (25 to 38) mm, which depicts qualitatively how much the microbial colonies inhibited drastically with prolonged sunlight irradiation. (Fig. S3 shows the zone inhibtion of K. pneumonia and S. aureus as an example)

Furthermore, the materials' minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) was evaluated under dark and sunlight modes to determine their antimicrobial activity quantitatively (The absorptions of strains at different materials concentrations diluted from 1000 to 3.9 μg/mL are shown in Table S1). As shown in Table 3 and under dark conditions, MIC concentrations for ZnO, CNDs, and Hybrid ranged to be (31.5–125) µg/mL, (62.5–250) µg/mL, and (15.6–62.5) µg/mL, respectively. Whereas under sunlight mode, MIC results of examined strains subjected to sunlight mode were more inhibited than dark mode, e.g., ZnO with a MIC concentration ranged from (15.6 to 62.5) µg/mL, and for CNDs, its MIC concentration ranged from (31.5 to 125) µg/mL. However, hybrid material shows an outstanding performance, and its efficiency approved by its MIC concentration ranged from (7.8 to 31.5) µg/mL. These findings prove that CNDs inseparably bound to ZnO particles with a synergistic effect enhancing the photocatalytic effect.

Moreover, ZnO, CNDs, and hybrid were also shown to alter the antimicrobial activity by its colonies forming units’ reduction under dark and sunlight modes, as shown in Table S2 (Number of colonies in absence and presence of antimicrobial agents), S3 (Percent colonies reduction), (Fig. S4: Photo of colony forming units of E. coli under light and dark before and after treating with synthesized materials), and Fig. 7 . The resulting colonies forming unit (CFU) reduction in microbial growth was represented as Log10 CFU and inhibition growth percentage, where ZnO under dark conditions gave a Log10 CFU ranging from (0.04 to 0.14) and a growth inhibition percentage (8–28%). However, ZnO nanoparticles exhibited higher antimicrobial activity in sunlight irradiation with a Log10 CFU ranging from (0.13 to 0.4) and inhibition growth (36–78%) (Fig. 7a). For CNDs (Fig. 7b), in the absence of irradiation light mode, it shows a Log10 CFU (0.02–0.18) and an inhibition growth (4–24%), but a noticeable activity inhibition growth with a Log10 CFU (0.08- 0.4) and inhibition growth (16–60%) while irradiated with photo-light. Even though ZnO materials still show better inhibitory ability when compared to CNDs under sunlight, indicating different ROS mechanism routes [27]. Also, hybrid nanocomposite (Fig. 7c) shows effective log CFU and inhibition growth percentage compared to both hybrid forming moieties, and its efficiency varies between dark and sunlight conditions, where dark mode shows a Log10 CFU (1.55–2.49) and inhibition growth (97.2–99.6%). In contrast, photo light irradiation enhanced microbial reduction with a Log10 CFU (2.09–4.39) and inhibition growth (99–99.99%). These findings depict how the combination of materials can effectively play a vital role in antimicrobial activity.

Furthermore, kill time kinetics of microbial inhibition of ZnO, CNDs, and hybrid nanocomposites, within the context of MIC, was quantified by monitoring microbial growth in different periods and incubated within a study period of 8 h at room temperature while the number of microbial were counted. The test was carried out under dark and sunlight modes and the results are presented in Fig. 8 . It can be seen that under (Fig. 8a); ZnO particles exhibited a marked inhibitory effect range from 3 to 6 h. Whereas, CNDs and hybrid demonstrate an inhibitory effect ranging from 2 to 4 h. In contrast, a marked inhibitory effect was observed on microbial growth under sunlight (Fig. 8b). That is, ZnO and CNDs exhibited somehow the same inhibitory microbial growth, ranging from 1 h to 3 h. Even more drastic antimicrobial enhancement can be seen with hybrid material which significantly suppressed microbial growth within 1 to 2 h. One can see that the number of microbial colonies decreased drastically within a limited period; this is ascribed to the synergistic effect in ambient sunlight mode that facilitated the production of ROS due to interfacial charge transfer from CND to ZnO and the penetration to the microbial membrane [60].

The antimicrobial activity mechanism of synthesized materials was examined by a glutathione deficiency test under dark and sunlight modes to investigate the role of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in microbial inhibition. As shown in Fig. 9 , the dark mode gives a glutathione deficiency percentage for hybrid (88.2%), CNDs (83%), and ZnO (62%), where ZnO has the lowest percentage indicating that its photocatalytic activity is restricted due to narrow UV–vis absorption spectrum and its large energy band gap which make it more active under UV irradiation [73]. In contrast, a remarkable fraction of glutathione was oxidized upon exposure to photo light irradiation as clear in Fig. 8 , for hybrid (95%), CNDs (85.5%), and ZnO (81.1%), respectively. Herein, the hybrid nanocomposite shows the highest oxidation percentage indicating the ROS synergist effect of combined moieties.

Mechanistically, the foremost step involves the photo generation of electron-hole pairs, and the peak position moves to a high field after exposure to the visible region [75]. It also comes with other research results of synergist process benefits of CNDs and ZnO in improving the separation efficiency of photo-generated electron pairs and the absorption and transformation of visible and UV lights.

Interestingly, the synthesized hybrid in the present work shows a broad microbial inhibition toward all tested microbes’ strains such as E. coli, S. aureus, K. pneumonia, MRSA, P. aeruginosa, Mold, S. cerevisiae, and A. fumigatus under dark and visible (sunlight) modes proving the enhancement occurs on ZnO upon hybridization with CNDs related to activity in the visible region. The as-prepared hybrid has been compared with other reported hybrid composites, and different CNDs sources were used for synthesizing hybrid composites (ZnO and CNDs); these composites were tested for their antimicrobial activity towards specific microbial strains and under one selected variable condition demonstrating variable MIC values as shown in Table 4 below:

ZnO–CNDs hybrid nanocomposite has been successfully prepared from ZnO particles and CNDs derived from olive solid wastes by the sol-gel precipitation method. The prepared hybrid was characterized through different techniques to find that it has the combination of features of both ZnO and CNDs with oxygen functional groups that enhance the dispersity in water in addition to an increase in negative charge of ζ-potential to −28 mV. The resulting hybrid showed a small energy band gap (2.8 eV) compared with ZnO (3.1 eV), improving its photocatalytic activity. It was obvious, that the hybrid acted as an efficient antimicrobial agent toward different gram-positive, gram-negative bacterial and fungal strains either in dark or sunlight testing modes, showing a lower minimum inhibitory concentration in dark (15.6–62.5 µg/ml) and in sunlight (7.8–31.5 µg/ml) compared with both CNDs and ZnO and exhibited high zone inhibition and colony forming units’ reduction with values reached to 99.9%. The wide spectrum antimicrobial activity of hybrid is directly correlated to its ROS-forming ability measured by Glutathione oxidation which was about (88.2%) and (95%) under dark and sunlight testing modes, respectively.
